criteria.label: State the heap invariant and the parent/child index formulas.
criteria.example: Min-heap invariant: `a[parent(i)] <= a[i]` for every i > 0. Index math: `parent(i) = (i-1)//2`, `left(i) = 2*i+1`, `right(i) = 2*i+2`. Quick check on `[1,3,5,4,8]`: every parent is ≤ its children → valid.

criteria.label: Use heapq.heappush, heappop, heapify, nlargest, nsmallest correctly.
criteria.example:
```python
import heapq
h = [3,1,4,1,5]
heapq.heapify(h)          # O(n)
heapq.heappush(h, 2)      # O(log n)
heapq.heappop(h)          # smallest; O(log n)
heapq.nlargest(3, h)      # ≈ O(n log k)
```

criteria.label: Implement top-k with a size-k heap and heapreplace.
Use negation (or a tie-breaker) to model max-heap semantics safely.
criteria.example: Python only has min-heaps. For top-K by score: push `(-score, tie_breaker, item)`; the `tie_breaker` (e.g. a monotonically increasing counter) avoids comparing `item`s when scores tie.

criteria.label: Pick a heap for priority queues, top-k, beam search and Dijkstra/A*.
criteria.example: Beam search keeping the K best partial sequences → min-heap of `(-logprob, seq)`. Dijkstra → priority queue keyed by tentative distance. RAG re-rank → `heapq.nlargest(K, candidates, key=score)`.

criteria.label: Distinguish heap O(log n) push/pop from O(n) bottom-up heapify.
criteria.example: Calling `heapify(list_of_n)` is O(n) — bottom-up sift-down is amortised linear. Doing `n` separate `heappush` calls is O(n log n). For a one-shot build from an existing list, `heapify` is strictly better.
